steps.title: Read what the two halves say
steps.content: First half: the letter was addressed to a local merchant, Henry Cullen. Second half (after the blank): "though, Bolivar's goal was to persuade political leaders from England and Europe to support his cause." The word "though" already signals a contrast with the surface fact, so the transition needs to introduce the deeper, end-state purpose behind the letter.

steps.title: Pick the transition that names a final purpose
steps.content: A word meaning "in the end" or "when all is said and done" fits: the merchant was the named recipient, but the real, ultimate aim was to sway European political leaders.

steps.title: Eliminate by rule
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'additionally,': signals another item in a list, but persuading political leaders is not an extra audience added to Cullen — it is the underlying purpose. Misread of the relationship. ✗
  • Choice B — 'ultimately,': marks the final, real goal behind the surface fact — exactly the move from named recipient to true intended audience. ✓
  • Choice C — 'accordingly,': signals an action taken in line with a prior reason. Nothing in the first half gives a cause that Bolivar's real goal follows from. Overreach to causation. ✗
  • Choice D — 'consequently,': also cause-effect — the goal would have to be a result of addressing Cullen, which the passage does not claim. Misread. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
